speaker: interviewee
text: And my uncle and Aunt Betty came back the same day, and-- and my other cousin, the younger one. They went to America. I don't really remember when-- them going off. All I have in my mind right now is the arrival, and their looking so awful. I don't remember anything else, because at that time, preparations were made for my emigration. And there were thousands of people applying for visas. And everybody was trying to get out of Germany at that-- after Crystal Night, it just opened Pandora's box. Everybody said, now it's-- we've got to get out. And the consulates, they were very overcrowded, and everybody was trying to get out. My mother said, you would do better on your own than my going with you, so she got-- ordered a taxi, told the taxi driver to take me to have my passport photo taken and to go to that and that organization and told me what to do. And as I was not too tall, she said, and if there are lots of people, just crawl to the front. [LAUGHS] And I did. As I told you, I was rather fresh.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was put in that taxi. And I was sitting in the back, the first time in my life sitting in a taxi by myself. And the driver-- she must have known him-- drove me to have my passport photo. And he sat there with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he drove me to the organization. I don't remember where. On the second floor, there were thousands of people. And I said, excuse me. I'm looking for my mother. [LAUGHS] Excuse me. I'm looking for my mother. And I went to the front and I got my stamp [LAUGHS] very quickly, went down to the taxi, and he took me back home. I got my permission. I got this-- I have my passport here. It had a big J on it. I must have had the passport. That's wrong. I needed a new photo for the visa. I-- I don't remember the details. All I remember is the feeling of I'm looking for my mother and being very, very [LAUGHS] fresh about it. I have a passport because as children, we would go on holiday every year. And we went to Denmark. I don't know whether that is true or not. My memory-- it's not on that passport, so it must have been a new passport. It has a J and a-- and a swastika on it, I know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I came back having achieved my-- [LAUGHS] my mission and being very, very proud of that. And that was the day my other uncle was killed in the concentration camp. The day I was due to leave-- my cousins had already left on the first transport. I was earmarked for the second.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were only allowed to take one suitcase. And everything was too big. We went shopping, and I said, that's too big. It doesn't fit. And all I remember mother saying, it has to last. We won't have any money in England, always with the expectations that everybody will follow. So nothing fitted, but it was put in the suitcase. And I remember wanting to take a doll. You're too old for dolls. So I really wasn't a child any longer. I really was a companion for my mother. And I shared a lot with her. I became an instant adult, little adult. That is something Hitler did to you. We lost a childhood. We really lost the-- the childhood. Then I-- then she took me to the-- late at night, she took me to the train station. Parents, hundreds of children there seemed to be. I don't know if there were hundreds. And the parents there, we were given numbers. And with our little suitcases, we were separated from our parents or from the other relatives and put into a train carriage, which was dark. Now, A, it was very cold. It was December. B, we were pretty frightened. Having to carry that suitcase with a number around your neck and crying, and the pandemonium was horrible. And we sat it seemed, again for about an hour waiting for the train to be loaded up. I don't remember any adults, only children. And we sat there in silence. And then slowly, it pulled out. And there was crying. And the goodbyes were just horrendous. Mother-- I didn't really mind. I'd been to a children's home before in the Black Forest. And my father had gone. And there was really a hope and an expectation that she would join us very soon. And, of course, I had my father to look forward to, which most of the other children didn't have.

speaker: interviewer
text: So eventually, being night, it was-- the lights went on. But there was silence. And I remember shivering. And in the other side of-- there were two-- there were benches opposite each other-- we had our luggage.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS kept on coming and checking on us and looking at our passports. Passports, please. You know, very abrupt, frightening, intimidating kind of things. I didn't know what to expect. I remember being scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: There opposite on the bench was a little boy. He looked like an old man. And he had one of those berets, those French berets on. And I remember somebody saying, it's warm in here. Why don't you take your hat off?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he just lifted the rim of the beret. And he had the same haircut as my cousin Eric. And otherwise-- he must have been nearly 17. The oldest that was taken out on the Kindertransporter was 17, so he must have come from the-- from the concentration camp. They did smuggle some of the older children out. Anyway, we traveled to Holland. And as the train got slower and slower to the borders of the Dutch border, the Nazis came in to inspect again and to be very officious. And then we saw some Dutch ladies and some Dutch officials. And from the silence-- I remember the silence in the train. And then as slowly the train went over to the Dutch border, there was jubilation. That I remember very clearly. Who was in the carriage or any other bodies, any other children, I don't remember at all. And then we were given some hot chocolate and we were given some rolls of some kind and led to the boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember going onto the ferry. And I'm not very-- a good sailor. And I looked and saw the water underneath and I felt cold. I was nauseous. I just remember that feeling of, oh, Lord. Now what?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the ferry took off over to across the British channel, the English Channel. And everybody was seasick. Everybody was seasick, including me. And I had a younger child in the bunk. So I went to the top bunk and kept her downstairs, down on the lower bunk. And both of us were so seasick. And then the-- the steward came in. And of course, I didn't-- I spoke German. He didn't speak English-- he only spoke English. And he shoved me back and said, get in there. It was a mess. The next morning, we got to England. And it had snowed. It was bitterly cold. And we were just taken off the-- you know, off the boat to a holiday camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: The British-- can you imagine one transport after the other coming to England? And the policy was, get the children out. Whatever you do, get the children out. Well, the British had to prepare. So we arrived in Dover. We're taken to a holiday camp without heating. There were little Nissen huts.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only one place was warm, and that was the cafeteria. It was a big hall. And they had fireplaces. And in turn, we would go and warm ourself up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there were my four cousins. And I remember-- all I remember is saying, how did you get so fat so fast? You know, when you're 11 years old, you have a strange sense of humor. I still do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she-- they had every stitch of clothing, one on top of the other. Everything from their suitcase was on their bodies. It was so cold, because the huts were not heated. And then a [INAUDIBLE] gave the-- sent hot water bottles for the children and Wellington boots for the snow, you know, rubber-- rubber overshoes. But we stayed in Dovercourt. I was lucky. My father didn't get the visa from my mother at that time in December, and-- but managed through this angel, Mrs. [? Marta, ?] to get foster homes for some children. So the four cousins were found foster homes. I was found a foster home, because he had to work and couldn't-- he had one room somewhere with another refugee lady.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I don't remember exactly how long we were at Dovercourt, two or three nights. And then we're put on the train. My father took all of us to Nottingham, up in the Midlands.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the twins were unidentical. One of them was very brazen like me, and the other one was very shy and heavy. And they were put into a foster home-- they'd never had children-- in Nottingham. And my father persuaded her to take both of them, not to separate the girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in a home in Nottingham. And, of course, I didn't speak English. And they didn't speak-- they spoke a little bit of Yiddish. They were Jewish. They were a niece of this Mrs. [? Marta. ?] And they really took me in because they needed a babysitter. They had a two-year-old, difficult child, and they wanted a helper.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: 11. And Adrienne was two. And, of course, I didn't speak English. And they thought I wasn't Jewish, because I didn't know anything about kashrut. And they said, what sort of a Jew are you? It was not a very happy situation. They took pity on me. But they also really took me because they needed a mother's helper, which I didn't mind. I love kids. And I'll tell you a story about Adrienne. They went out to the cinema, to the movies. And after a few days, I washed my hair and put a towel around my head. And usually, Adrienne didn't-- slept through the night and they were out. And she cried. And I went in there, and she screamed her head off. And I spoke German to her. That's all I knew, a few English words. Now be quiet, go to sleep, sort of thing. And she screamed even louder. And then I bent down, and the towel fell off. And she started calming down. She didn't recognize me. And I cuddled her, and then I told her a German story, and she fell asleep. [LAUGHS] But I remember being sent to school right after Christmas in the new year, 1939, and being in the English school and having difficulty. But I learned. I wrote letters to my mother in Germany that mailboxes are-- are blue here in England, and you know, that sort of thing, you can-- this is different and this is different, in German. But by the time the spring semester came, I used, as I call it, [NON-ENGLISH]. I spoke and wrote English pretty well. It's unbelievable. I have the letters, so I can prove, you know, that the language was learned very quickly. But getting used to a different way of life and people feeling sorry for you and pitying you and looking down on you as the refugee, it was tough. Now, I didn't see the-- you know, I didn't see my cousins very much, nor my brother. So it was isolation.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your mother? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: She stayed behind. Visa was refused something like 11 times, until my father's partner went to the foreign office in London to see the original application. He insisted on that. And what had happened, my father's English wasn't that good. He put a tick in the wrong box, were you ever a Communist or some-- I don't even know the details. And he went through the application, saw the mistake, appealed and got all the information that he is now his partner and-- and she got out in August '39 only, just before the beginning of the war. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I stayed with the foster parents until Mother came over in August. I went to London. My father then got an apartment in London.

speaker: interviewee
text: Went to London at the end of August. Went to high school. I got a test. It was time for me to go to high school in London. Was tested out. First day in school was the 1st of September. First thing we were told is, here's the gas mask. Bring a suitcase tomorrow morning. We're going to be evacuated.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was home something like three days. Home-- I mean, I was together with my mother again and my father. And on the 2nd of September, the next day, we were gone. We were taken to the railroad station once again. And we were taken to the Midlands, because they were expecting to be-- the Germans to invade. They-- they were expecting London to be invaded. When you say 'we'--

speaker: interviewee
text: All the children--

speaker: interviewer
text: Again with the Kindertransport.

speaker: interviewee
text: --in the school. In the-- but these were British children.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: All the British children. But to me, it didn't make any difference, because they were all strangers anyhow. But it was the second year of high school. You know, I took the test, and they put me into the second year of high school. And we were put in the train. We got to Northampton, a place in the Midlands. And there again, we were put into a big hall. It's like deja vu for the second time. And the English children, of course, had never had that experience. Then we were taken in groups of 10 or 15. And each street was taken by a teacher or a warden. And then they blew the whistle, and everybody who had a spare room or a spare bed had to take in these evacuees. And they would come out. This was like a cattle market. I'll take that one and I'll take that one. Well, to me, it didn't make a damn bit of difference. I didn't know one from the other. And they took us in. They didn't have beds. They had a chair, two armchairs put together, and a couch. And I didn't know the girl. And they were working. And the next morning, out of every house, these evacuees and these children came. And then we heard a whistle and an air raid siren. War had-- had been declared. That was the 3rd of September. And we-- my parents, being enemy aliens, couldn't move and couldn't visit me. And I couldn't go back to London with the bombing and so on. And that foster home-- I had six foster homes, from one to the other, for some reason or other. They had to be placed. So, you know, for some reason or other we were moved. The other girl, Jean, went back to London. Her mother came and picked her up. But I couldn't be picked up. I had five or six foster homes. I only remember three, but I know there were some in between. And we had to share the school with the children of Northampton, so there wasn't much education there either.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in contact, though, with your parents? Were you able to be in contact at this time?

words.text: to be in contact at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: We wrote. Yeah. There was-- there was communication. But I'll tell you one story. One of my foster parents had a older daughter who was married to an RAF or Air Force pilot. And I came home from school, and she nearly killed me. You bloody German! You killed my husband! I didn't know what the heck she was talking about. Her husband was killed over Hamburg, no less. He was shot down. She was pregnant. And she was in a state of shock. She just had the telegram. And it was my fault, you know, that Jack was killed, in all of all places. My foster mother had to take her hands off my neck. So from being a refugee, Jewish refugee, I became a Jewish refugee evacuee. And then I became a German, an enemy-- enemy alien. And they let you know it, too, that you were German. You became the enemy alien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I basically only mixed with foster parents at that time, I-- except for the Jewish family who-- in Nottingham, whom I didn't care for at all. I was always among strangers from one to the other. Were there other Jewish children in this-- in this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Other than my cousins, I don't really remember. I was in a state of shock. I did all the right things. I was grateful. I was thankful. I was-- I was intimidated, really. I was brought up to be obedient and to be good and, you know, Germanic. You do as you're told. And my father, before he left in '36, gave me a little talk, a father-daughter talk. And he said, you know, you know the difference between right and wrong, and we expect you to do it. And there was always a little Daddy sitting on my shoulder when I-- my rambunctiousness sort of and my cheekiness would come out. And he would sort of say, remember. [LAUGHS] And that sort of was a tremendous burden all my life. I was never really young. I had to toe the line, do what was the right thing to do. I still have a hard time being naughty or doing things just for fun because of that. And I think you can empathize with that one. We were never really young. Anyway, I went back to London. And I had decided I'd go on strike. I wanted an education. I wanted to get my matric, which was the graduation from high school. And I called my father and mother and said, I'm coming home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year is this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1934-3-31-1941. So I was away for two and a half years. And the doodlebugs were still on and there were still incendiary bombs. And I said I just didn't care. I wanted to be with them. I wanted to get some education. And I was an air raid warden with my father. And my mother was working with the WVS, the women's soup kitchens and, you know, really with the British.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother stayed in Nottingham, because he was in high school. And he got his-- he stayed with that Mrs. [? Marta's ?] relatives. My brother's a very different, accepting kind of a person. I was more rambunctious. And I kicked more than he did. He accepted things very calmly. He has a different personality and doesn't remember very much. Now he's in England, he does not recall very much at all. He never realized that I came on the Kindertransport. And this is something that's fascinating. You're so-- you were so intimidated. You were so insecure all your young life. You had all your energy to preserve to keep ahead and really didn't have much insight into other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I now-- came where I'm sitting here right now in Los Angeles, staying with a friend from Hamburg who was living next to us, I really don't know her story. I know that she went to America eventually, and her father came, and so on. Her mother came to England. That I remember, because my father got her out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she, Rutha's mother, could only come out on a domestic-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, there were different categories, different visas. And whatever was available as a job were domestics and nurses, assistant nurses.

speaker: interviewer
text: And my aunt with the four children, the mother of the four children, was very, very wealthy. She got her husband out of concentration camp, somehow or other, to Latin America somewhere, where he emigrated. Chile or-- I'm not quite sure where. Uruguay? And then at the last moment, just before the beginning of the war, my father got her a job as a domestic in London. So the rest of the family, then? Your--

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost my aunt. I-- my grandmother refused to stay behind. I loved my grandmother. She felt that both her boys had families, and she was too old to move. You don't transplant an old tree, she would say. I'm staying. And she stayed. And she was-- she was killed, as was my aunt, who was a nurse in Berlin. And she felt it was her obligation to stay with patients in the Jewish hospital. And eventually, she-- and-- was taken to Riga and was killed there. And my uncles also went to different concentration camps, my extended family. But the nuclear family escaped, luckily. And England really accepted the refugees with open arms, to the great extent. We became accepted like the British, in schools and in the evacuation. And it has to be mentioned, I think, because I don't think it is known how decent the English were to the refugees. And thousands of them went to England.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Zionists couldn't-- the Hakhsharot people couldn't go to Palestine, so England took her in-- took groups of these Zionist groups in with the Kindertransports. So England was very generous. And I became British. And I'm still British. I felt had it not been, who knows? [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: That takes us to the war. And my parents were bombed out. At one point, they had incendiary bombs and so on, so, you know, it wasn't all glorious. There was food rationing, of course. It's amazing how little food you can do with, or what you can do without. And with a Germanic upbringing, where you save everything anyway, that and a good combination, the rationing was pretty good. It really was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the war was finished, the VE. Oh, no. I went to high school. I got my matriculation. And we could not go to college straight from school. It was still wartime, so you had to do wartime service, the high school graduates.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I wasn't sure-- I wanted to go into medicine at that time, but had to do some wartime work, and I went into a wartime day nursery and was put in with the real infants on the second floor-- and the doodlebugs. The doodlebugs were unmanned bombs sent. And they were not spotted by the radar.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you could-- there were V-1s and V-2s. V-1s had-- you had no chance. You just heard them go, whee, and explode. The V-2 would come, whee, and they got fainter and fainter and fainter. And you hoped and prayed that it wouldn't stop, because once it stopped, it was overhead. And I was responsible on the second floor for infants. And I remember hearing a doodlebug, and there were three infants. And it was on the second floor. And the dilemma of what to do with three infants-- I tucked one under the arm there, and I had one like this and had to go downstairs. And the air raid shelters were in the street, away from glass. So I was carrying these three infants.

speaker: interviewer
text: The door was open downstairs, because the-- the older ones were downstairs. And I had to take those infants to the shelter. That sort of thing sticks, the triage kind of thing-- which one do you pick up? So I became a nursery nurse for that year, just about a year.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the girl who said to me in Hamburg I'll teach you Hebrew was very Orthodox. I registered for college. Oh, [? Hannah ?] was a twin. And you know there was a coming and a going in Hamburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: I-- we had no idea when they didn't come to school, were they incarcerated? Were their fathers gone? Where were they? Were they ill? Nobody had time to ask these questions.

speaker: interviewee
text: I went down to register for college, for training college for-- to be a teacher. I was going down to register, and who came up but [? Hannah? ?] So we were together in college for three years and became very good friends. And we studied together, and she taught-- I learned one thing, not only her kindness at that time, which I remember to this day. But I went home with her to learn Hebrew. And there were six brothers and sisters and the grandmother, who was hard of hearing with the [NON-ENGLISH]. I learned to respect the Orthodox Jew. I learned to go into that area where the Orthodox Jews lived to begin with and the shtreimel and whatever else, you know, the pious-- and I had tremendous respect. And it was really rubbing shoulders with Orthodox Jews for the first time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, this is in England?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then again when I met [? Hannah, ?] we became very close friends. And her mother got out also, and her father. And we became good friends in London. And then, when later on, after the war, I was a teacher, as I said, the-- after the war, the displaced persons came to England, after-- the concentration camp survivors. I felt it my duty to help teach English and to integrate and to help there and did some tutoring for the DPs. [? Hannah ?] at that time went to Israel. I became very involved with the displaced persons. And I realized then that the English were not as kindly to the DPs as they were to us as children, as poor refugees in-- in the '30s. They had suffered. They had had a war. They had had losses. They had lost their property, and they'd lost their men. And I felt it was for Israel to take the DPs in, so I went to Israel with a group of DPs. So I guess my Zionistic background from my mother somehow also influenced me indirectly.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- what year did you go to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '50, early '50.
